does: This thesis develops constrained reinforcement learning algorithms and theory across three settings. It introduces ACPO (Average-Constrained Policy Optimization) for infinite-horizon average-cost CMDPs, combining sensitivity analysis with trust-region methods to handle constraints stably while providing theoretical guarantees and empirical performance improvements. For episodic constraints, it presents e-COP, the first policy optimization method for episodic CMDPs built on an episodic policy difference lemma, offering provable guarantees with simplicity and scalability. The work extends to preference learning via warmPref-PS, a posterior sampling strategy for linear bandits that integrates offline heterogeneous human preference data into online learning by explicitly modeling rater competence, addressing challenges in learning from diverse annotators.
connects: This work builds on the foundations of constrained reinforcement learning and policy optimization, extending classical Lagrangian methods and trust-region approaches like TRPO and PPO to handle complex constraint structures. It connects to the broader literature on CMDPs dating back to Altman's work, addressing limitations of prior methods in stability and scalability. The preference learning component roots itself in recent advances in learning from human feedback and reinforcement learning from human preferences (RLHF), which have become crucial for aligning large language models. The explicit modeling of annotator heterogeneity extends work on crowdsourcing and preference aggregation, responding to practical challenges observed in real alignment efforts where human raters have different expertise levels and biases.
enables: These contributions set up multiple future directions in safe and aligned AI systems. The constrained RL framework enables deployment in safety-critical domains where multiple competing objectives must be balanced with formal guarantees. ACPO and e-COP provide foundational tools for practitioners building controllable RL systems with verifiable constraint satisfaction. The preference learning methodology opens avenues for more robust human alignment of large language models by better handling diverse and noisy human feedback signals. Together, the work enables development of RL systems that are simultaneously effective, safe, and properly aligned with heterogeneous human values—critical capabilities for deploying advanced AI systems where safety constraints and preference alignment are paramount.
